div.head: PE–49. Telegram from the Chargé in Peru (Neal) to the Department of StateSource: Department of State, Central Files, 723.13/9–260. Confidential; Priority.
div.p: 163. Prime Minister Beltran called me in yesterday to discuss his possible resignation and the question of his attending Bogotá conference.Reference is to the third meeting of the Committee of 21 at Bogotá, September 5–13. For documentation, see Documents ETA–32–34, 38, 42, 44, 47–49, 55; GT–31; HA–29; NI–11.

He was at very low ebb mainly, I feel, because of overwork on interpellation and was hurt by coldness with which Senate personally treated him. Said he was reluctant to continue in Cabinet and wanted my opinion.

I told him our polls indicated assurance final vote favorable and that APRA leader had only last night predicted his victory after personal abuse. I said opposition playing politics with him and his friends were hesitant to publicly indicate their feeling but vote would tell.In telegram 171 from Lima, September 5, the Embassy reported that Prime Minister Beltrán received a confidence vote of 78 to 29 in the Chamber of Deputies on September 4 following in interpellation, (formal questioning of [illegible in the original] policy in the legislature) begun on September 2, during which he answered 41 questions by the opposition Partido Democráta-Cristiano on his program. (723.13/9–560)

I gave him assurance which I expect he was looking for that if he and other members of his Cabinet (I had in mind Minister of Development Grieve and Minister of Agriculture Zarak) are forced out all good will and loans he generated for Peru on recent U.S. visit will be nullified.In telegram 147 to Lima, September 7, the Department of State expressed sympathy with the Embassy’s efforts to encourage Prime Minister Beltrán, but added that “were he to resign, there would be no question of nullifying credit authorizations to Peruvian Government except under extraordinary circumstances fundamentally altering U.S.-Peruvian relations.” (723.13/9–260) Informed him I had made this clear to other responsible leaders in government.

As for Bogotá, told him it essential he attend because this will be opportunity for him recoup Peruvian prestige lost by lack of leadership in Peruvian delegation at MFM San Jose.In despatch 161 from Lima, September 16, the Embassy reported that Foreign Minister Porras resigned effective September 16, stating in his letter of resignation published that day that his position of conciliation toward Cuba, which he assumed at the Seventh Meeting of Consultation of American Foreign Ministers at San José, diverged from instructions he received from his Government ordering the Peruvian delegation to support the Declaration of San José. (723.12/9–1660)

Am certain he feels better this morning after Senate victory reported following EMBTEL 164.In telegram 164 from Lima, September 2, the Embassy reported that Prime Minister Beltrán won a 26 to 13 confidence vote in the Senate on September 2 after an interpellation, begun on August 31, during which he answered 39 opposition questions on his program by the Acción Popular and the Partido Democráta-Cristiano. (723.13/9–260)
